title: Managing Sino-American Interdependence
gist: Qiyuan Xu argues the Trump-Xi summit signaled a shift from confrontation toward managed strategic stability, because both leaders now recognize that decoupling would damage their own firms, consumers, markets, and innovation ecosystems.

insights.claim: The Trump-Xi summit signaled that the US-China relationship is moving from intense confrontation back toward a 'constructive relationship of strategic stability,' with both sides committed to managing rather than escalating disputes.
insights.speaker: Qiyuan Xu
insights.so_what: A de-escalation framing supports risk-on positioning in China-exposed equities and supply chains, but watch whether 'managed' competition holds.
insights.quote: the Sino-American relationship is moving from intense confrontation back toward something more stable

insights.claim: Both Washington and Beijing have come to view continued escalation as costly, dangerous, and unsustainable, implying a structural ceiling on decoupling momentum.
insights.speaker: Qiyuan Xu
insights.so_what: If both sides see escalation as self-defeating, expect tariff/export-control rhetoric to outpace actual decoupling.
insights.quote: each has come to realize that continued escalation is costly, dangerous, and unsustainable

insights.claim: If either the US or China tries to cripple or decouple from the other, it will harm its own firms, consumers, financial markets, and innovation ecosystem, making full decoupling self-injurious.
insights.speaker: Qiyuan Xu
insights.so_what: The 'same boat' framing argues against tail-risk pricing of a hard US-China decoupling for cross-exposed multinationals.
insights.quote: If either the United States or China tries to cripple or decouple from the other, it will harm its own firms, consumers, financial markets, and innovation ecosystem.
insights.locator: Standfirst, 'they are in the same boat'
